cards.back: Both accept anything, but unknown forces a check before use; any disables checking.
interface extends/merges (object shapes); type also does unions, tuples, mapped types.
Freezes a literal to its narrowest readonly type — handy for unions/tuples.
Exhaustiveness checks: a missed union case fails to compile.
Checks (typeof, in, ===) shrink a union so each branch knows the precise type.
?. safely accesses maybe-null paths; ?? gives a default only for null/undefined.
They keep type info flowing: identity<T>(x:T):T preserves the caller's type.
Marks a property/array immutable at compile time.
typeof in a type context reads a value's type; a name can be both.
null/undefined stop being assignable to everything — handle them explicitly.
A value that can be one of several types: string | number.
A type that is one exact value, e.g. "GET" | "POST" instead of string.
Union of string literals is simpler and tree-shakeable; enums add runtime code.
A fixed-length array with known types per position: [string, number].
A function returning "x is T" that narrows a type for the compiler.
Built-in utility types that transform a type: make fields optional, keep, or drop them.
Gives a union of an object type's keys: keyof {a;b} is "a" | "b".
A type for an object with keys K and values V: Record<string, number>.
Tells TS a value isn't null/undefined — use sparingly, it can lie.
It overrides the checker without runtime checks — can hide real bugs. Prefer narrowing.
A union of objects sharing a literal tag (kind) so TS can narrow by it.
No — let TS infer obvious ones; annotate function params and public APIs.
void is the type of a function that returns nothing; undefined is a specific value.
